coredata.dc:title: Ingenious pH-sensitive etoposide loaded folic acid decorated mesoporous silica-carbon dot with carboxymethyl-βcyclodextrin gatekeeper for targeted drug delivery and imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Shirani, Marziyeh Poshteh
Rezaei, Behzad
Khayamian, Taghi
Dinari, Mohammad
Shamili, Fazileh Hosseini
Ramezani, Mohammad
Alibolandi, Mona
coredata.dc:description: A new strategy is reported for the synthesis of label-free fluorescent mesoporous silica (MS) by the introduction of fluorescent carbon dots in the MSs (MSCDs) in this work. Etoposide (ETO) loaded MSCDs have been used as a drug model. Carboxymethyl β-cyclodextrin (CβCD) used as a gatekeeper agent was attached to amine-functionalized MSCDs to retain ETO molecules inside the nanocarrier. In order to target the nanocarrier to the site of action, folic acid (FA) was grafted onto the MSCDs surface (FA-CβCD-MSCDs). The in vitro release of an entrapped ETO from the formulation in phosphate buffered saline (PBS) (pH 7.4) and citrate buffer (pH 5.4) was investigated. At neutral pH in PBS, the pores are blocked by CβCD which prevent premature ETO release. However, under the weakly acidic intercellular environment of the tumor, the amide bond can be partially hydrolyzed and consequently lead to the ETO release from the nanocarrier. The targeted and ETO-loaded FA-CβCD-MSCDs showed a higher growth inhibition towards FA-positive HeLa cells compared with FA-negative HepG2 cells, as demonstrated by comparison of in vitro cytotoxicity experiments. In addition, the CDs emission was used for the fluorescent microscopic imaging. Moreover, molecular docking and molecular dynamics simulations (MDS) were applied to examine the interactions of ETO molecules with the topoisomerase II (Top II). ETO molecules bind Top II with overall binding constants of 3.08 × 1010 M−1, according to docking results. Based on MDS results, ETO-Top II complex is formed through hydrophobic interactions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Etoposide
Mesoporus silica
Molecular modeling
Bioimaging
Folic acid
Gatekeeper

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Peroration of mesosilica nanoparticles coated carbon dots (MSCDs)
Preparation and characterization of FA-CβCD-MSCDs nanoparticles
Drug loading and in vitro release

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors wish to thank Isfahan University of Technology (IUT) Research Council and Center of Excellence in Sensor and Green Chemistry for their support. We gratefully acknowledge the Sheikh Bahaei National High Performance Computing of the Isfahan University of Technology for performing the parallel simulation on RAKHSH cluster. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic presentation of the pH-responsive FA-CβCD-MSCDs nanocarrier for controlled of targeted drug delivery and bioimaging.
(A) TEM image of CDs, (B) DLS analysis of the CDs, (C) and (D) TEM images of FA-CβCD-MSCDs-ETO.

FT-IR spectra in the range of 400–4000 cm−1MSCDs, CβCD-MSCDs and FA-CβCD-MSCDs, (B) TGA curves of, amine-MSCDs, CβCD-MSCDs and FA-CβCD-MSCDs, (C) Low angle powder XRD of amine-MSCDs and FA-CβCD-MSCDs and (D) Fluorescence spectrum of CDs, MSCDs, ETO, and ETO loaded CβCD-MSCDs.
The release profile of FA-CβCD-MSCDs, (A) PBS, pH 7.4 and (B) citrate buffer pH 5.4.

(A) HeLa cells viability after incubation with different amounts of CDs and FA-CβCD-MSCDs for 48 h (n = 3). (B) HepG2 cells viability after incubation with different amounts of CDs and FA-CβCD-MSCDs for 48 h (n = 3). (C) HeLa cell viability cultured with ETO, CβCD-MSCDs-ETO, FA- CβCD-MSCDs-ETO after 48 h (n = 3). (D) HepG2 cell viability cultured with ETO, CβCD-MSCDs-ETO, FA- CβCD-MSCDs-ETO after 48 h (n = 3).
Fluorescence microscopy image of cells 3 h after exposure to FA- CβCD-MSCDs-ETO (A) HeLa cells and (B) HepG2 cells.

(A) The molecular docking of the Top II with the two ETO molecules by UCSF chimera, (B) two-dimensional interactions made using LIGPLOT+, (C) Molecular dynamics simulation of the Top II consists of two ETO molecules using UCSF chimera, and (D) The two-dimensional interactions generated by LIGPLOT+ in time 10,000 ps.
The zeta potential and DLS of the nanoparticles in water PBS and DMEM.

head.ce:title: Ingenious pH-sensitive etoposide loaded folic acid decorated mesoporous silica-carbon dot with carboxymethyl-βcyclodextrin gatekeeper for targeted drug delivery and imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Isfahan University of Technology, Isfahan 84156-83111, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Immunology, School of Medicine, Mashhad University of Medical Sciences, Mashhad, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pharmaceutical Research Center, Pharmaceutical Technology Institute, Mashhad University of Medical Sciences, Mashhad, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Biotechnology, School of Pharmacy, Mashhad University of Medical Sciences, Mashhad, Iran

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Present address: Department of Chemistry, Isfahan University of Technology, Isfahan 84156-83111, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new strategy is reported for the synthesis of label-free fluorescent mesoporous silica (MS) by the introduction of fluorescent carbon dots in the MSs (MSCDs) in this work. Etoposide (ETO) loaded MSCDs have been used as a drug model. Carboxymethyl β-cyclodextrin (CβCD) used as a gatekeeper agent was attached to amine-functionalized MSCDs to retain ETO molecules inside the nanocarrier. In order to target the nanocarrier to the site of action, folic acid (FA) was grafted onto the MSCDs surface (FA-CβCD-MSCDs). The in vitro release of an entrapped ETO from the formulation in phosphate buffered saline (PBS) (pH 7.4) and citrate buffer (pH 5.4) was investigated. At neutral pH in PBS, the pores are blocked by CβCD which prevent premature ETO release. However, under the weakly acidic intercellular environment of the tumor, the amide bond can be partially hydrolyzed and consequently lead to the ETO release from the nanocarrier. The targeted and ETO-loaded FA-CβCD-MSCDs showed a higher growth inhibition towards FA-positive HeLa cells compared with FA-negative HepG2 cells, as demonstrated by comparison of in vitro cytotoxicity experiments. In addition, the CDs emission was used for the fluorescent microscopic imaging. Moreover, molecular docking and molecular dynamics simulations (MDS) were applied to examine the interactions of ETO molecules with the topoisomerase II (Top II). ETO molecules bind Top II with overall binding constants of 3.08 × 1010 M−1, according to docking results. Based on MDS results, ETO-Top II complex is formed through hydrophobic interactions.

ce:section.ce:para: Although great progress has been made regarding cancer treatment, cancer is still a major human health threat [1, 2]. There are some disadvantages in the conventional chemotherapy of tumors, for instance, poor targeting and severe side effects on normal tissues. Drug delivery systems (DDSs) whose ultimate objective is releasing significant amounts of the drug in selected areas without harming the surrounding healthy cells have been developed to overcome these drawbacks [3, 4]. Different DDSs including liposomes, dendrimers, hydrogels and inorganic nanoparticles [5–8] have been studied and developed as emerging platforms for cancer chemotherapy in the past decades. Since their introduction as drug carriers by Vallet-Regi et al. in 2001 [9], DDSs based on mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MSNs) have been widely used in drug carrier systems. Excellent biocompatibility and low toxicity make MSNs appropriate for in vivo applications. Furthermore, MSNs have a high drug loading ability because of the large surface area and pore volume [10–14].

The gatekeepers, which are used to prevent premature drug release, are important parts of MSN based DDSs. In general, gatekeepers are nanoparticles or bulky molecules, which block the pore entrances [15] and are affected by changes in pH or temperature, redox potentials, enzymes or photoirradiation to release drugs on demand [16–20]. Various molecules, including polymers, CdS quantum dots, Fe3O4, and tannin have previously been reported as gatekeepers, which intelligently control the release of the encapsulated molecules from the silica pores [21–25]. β-cyclodextrin (βCD), which is a truncated-cone polysaccharide mainly made of seven d-glucose monomers linked by α-1,4-glucose bonds, has a hydrophilic exterior and an interior hydrophobic cavity. Water solubility of βCD is poor. However, many modified βCDs such as carboxymethyl β-cyclodextrin (CβCD) have high water solubility. Therefore, native and modified βCDs have been widely used in many fields such as supramolecular and medicinal chemistry [26, 27]. In the current study, βCD was employed as a capping agent to block the MSP pores. βCD, with a suitable ring size, matches the MSN pore diameters making it appropriate gatekeeper or pore blocker [28].

New frontiers in simultaneous tracing and treatment of cancer have been opened by designing strategies for integrating imaging modalities and drug transport. Carbon dots (CDs) are the modern members of the luminescent carbon nanomaterial family [29]. The bright fluorescence, high solubility, high chemical inertness, low toxicity and easy functionalization of carbon dots in comparison with conventional dyes and toxic semiconductor quantum dots make them considerably important [30, 31]. Given their outstanding properties, carbon dots can be promptly applied in bioimaging applications.

Etoposide (ETO) is an epipodophyllotoxin antineoplastic agent, which functions by creating a ternary complex with topoisomerase II (Top II) and DNA, bringing about DNA breaks and cell death [32]. Given its low solubility, poor bioavailability and drug resistance, DDS should be urgently engineered to vanquish these disadvantages and improve the clinical therapy effect [33–35].

In addition, one of the influential obstacles in cancer chemotherapy is the undesirable side effects and systematic cytotoxicity corresponding to non-specific delivery of anticancer drugs. Thus, the progress of new strategies for the delivery of the therapeutic dosage of payload to the target site in an early manner is of utmost importance. Different ligands including aptamers, peptides, monoclonal antibodies, and small chemical molecules have already been used as targeting agents [36–38].

Folic acid (FA) is a small molecule (441-Da weight), which is stable over wide temperature and pH ranges, in addition to being cheap and non-immunogenic. It also maintains the ability to bind to the folate receptor after attachment to the diagnostic markers or drugs [39]. Having attached to the folate receptors (FRs) located within caveolae, FA is internalized through the endocytotic pathway.

A multi-functional antitumor DDS has been developed by the introduction of pH-responsive FA targeting CβCD-MSCDs, allowing the targeted delivery and controlled release of the loaded ETO into tumor cells, with simultaneous real time bio-imaging capability. In general, FA-CβCD-MSCDs nanosystems are inactive in normal cells (pH 7.4). Having reached the target tumor cells and tissues (pH 5.4), however, the hydrolysis of the amide bond between CβCD and MSCDs causes the faster release of encapsulated ETO. In addition to selective targeting and controlled drug delivery, FA-CβCD-MSCDs provides great potential for real time imaging in order to monitor cellular tracking during tumor therapy. Meanwhile, the binding of ETO molecules to Top II was surveyed by molecular docking and molecular dynamics simulations (MDSs) methods.

d-glucose, l-aspartic acid, 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) and N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N-ethyl carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) were obtained from Merck Chemical Co. Etoposide (≥99%), β-cyclodextrin (≥97%), mono chloroacetic acid (≥99%), folic acid (≥97%), N-cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), 3-[4,5-dimethyl-thiazol-2-yl]-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were supplied by Sigma Aldrich Chemical Co. All materials and solvents were of analytical grade and applied as received.

Human cervical cancer HeLa and liver cancer HepG2 cell lines were obtained from the National Cell Bank of Iran, Pasteur Institute of Iran. The cells were cultured in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's (DEME) medium supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS), 1% (v/v) penicillin and 1% (v/v) streptomycin at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere (95%) containing 5% CO2.

CDs were prepared as previously reported by Zheng and et al. [40]. 450 mg of glucose (2.5 mmol) and 333 mg of l-aspartic acid (2.5 mmol) were dissolved in 1 M NaOH (3 mL) under sonication. The solution was heated to 120 °C and retained at that temperature for 30 min. It was then heated to 200 °C and maintained at that temperature for 20 min. The product was dissolved in 20 mL of deionized (DI) water and then dialyzed (cut off: 3.5 kDa) for 24 h to remove small molecules.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Peroration of mesosilica nanoparticles coated carbon dots (MSCDs)
ce:section.ce:para: MSCDs were prepared by the sol-gel process with slight modifications [41]. 0.1 g of CTAB and 0.027 g of NaOH were dissolved in 50 mL of DI water and vigorously stirred to form a homogeneous solution. Subsequently, 600 μL of CDs synthesized were added to the solution. The mixture was heated up to 70 °C and stirred for 1 h. Subsequently, 400 μL of TEOS and 1 mL of ethyl acetate were added to the solution. The resulting solution was stirred for 3 h at 70 °C. The product was centrifuged and washed several times using ethanol and DI water. The product was dispersed in 160 mL of methanol and 9 mL of HCl to remove the surfactant template (CTAB). The suspension obtained was refluxed at 60 °C for 48 h. The MSCDs were collected through centrifugation, washed with ethanol and DI water repeatedly and dried in vacuo at 60 °C overnight. MSCDs surface amine modification was carried out by suspending nanoparticles (200 mg) in a solution of dry toluene (80 mL) with APTES (3 mL) and refluxing the resulting mixture under the N2 atmosphere at 120 °C for 24 h. The amine-MSCDs were together via filtration under reduced pressure, washed with toluene and dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C overnight.

1 mL of a 1 mg/mL stock solution of ETO in methanol was added to the MSCDs solution (20 mg) at pH 7.4 (PBS, 20 mL) and stirred overnight. The prepared formulation was centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 20 min. The supernatant was collected and the amount of free ETO in the supernatant was determined spectrofluorimetrically at 280 nm excitation using a standard ETO solution calibration curve. The encapsulation efficiency (EE) and drug loading (DL) of ETO loaded on to MSCDs were obtained as follows:
EE
%
=
Total weight of
ETO − Free
ETO
weight in supernatant
Total weight of
ETO
× 100
DL
%
=
Total weight of
ETO − Free
ETO
weight in supernatant
Mass of final formulation
× 100

The procedure for CβCD synthesis is presented as supporting information. EDC/NHS mediated amidation reaction was used to graft CβCD into amine-MSCDs. CβCD (10 mg) was dissolved in PBS buffer solution at pH 7.4 (10.0 mL) and the resulting solution was added into a centrifuge tube. EDC (20 mg) and NHS (10 mg) were added under stirring at room temperature into a tube over a period of 1 h to activate the carboxyl groups. Amine-MSCDs (20 mg) were then added into the solution and the resulting reaction mixture was stirred overnight. CβCD-MSCDs were finally obtained via centrifuging at 12000 rpm for 20 min and washed several times using DI water.

FA (5 mg) was suspended in PBS at pH 7.4 (10 mL) to attach FA to the CβCD-MSCDs surface. EDC (15.0 mg) and NHS (5.0 mg) were then added under constant stirring over a period of 1 h. Nanoparticles were subsequently added and the resulting reaction mixture was stirred overnight. To remove the unreacted starting materials, CβCD-MSCDs conjugates were subsequently dialyzed against distilled water for 24 h by a dialysis membrane (molecular cutoff: 3.5 kDa).

A Uwin UVD-295 spectrophotometer (USA) was used to carry out absorption measurements. A Shimadzu RF-5301 PC spectrofluorometer (Japan) was used to record fluorescence spectra. A Jasco 680-plus FT-IR spectrophotometer (Japan) was used to record Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra for wave numbers 4000–400 cm−1. A Malvern ZEN3600 dynamic light scattering (DLS) instrument (UK) was used to obtain the diameter and the particle dimension distribution of carbon dots (CDs). An Asenware AW-XDM300 (Chain) was used to carry out X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis. The appearance of CDs and ETO-loaded FA-CβCD-MSCDs were observed using a Philips CM30 300 kV transmission electron microscope (TEM). A SAM-X instrument (France) was applied to perform energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX). A STA503 TA instrument (Germany) was used to carry out thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) in a nitrogen atmosphere at a heating rate of 10 °C/min from ambient temperature to 800 °C. A Tanso Raman spectrophotometer (Iran) was used to record Raman spectra.

The dialysis membrane method was used to evaluate the drug release. 2 mL of ETO-loaded CβCD-MSCDs and ETO-loaded MSCDs were introduced into the dialysis bag (cutoff = 3.5 kDa) and the dialysis bag was immersed in PBS (pH = 7.4) and citrate buffer (pH = 5.4) and then placed in a shaker incubator set at 60 rpm at 37 °C. 2 mL aliquots of the release medium were removed at certain time intervals, adding the same volume of fresh medium. Spectrofluorometry was used to analyze the samples at the excitation of 280 nm. All assays were done in triplicate.

MTT [3-(4, 5-dimethyl thiazol-2-yl)-2, 5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide] assay was used to survey the cytotoxic effects of free ETO, ETO-loaded CβCD-MSCDs with or without FA, CDs, and blank FA-CβCD-MSCDs. Either HeLa or HepG2 cells were seeded into the 96-well plate (100 μL each well) and incubated at a density of 5 × 103 cells per well in DEME for 24 h. The cells were treated with free ETO, targeted and non-targeted CβCD-MSCDs-ETO (equivalent concentration of 0.2–100 μg/mL ETO). CDs and blank FA-CβCD-MSCDs were also tested at a concentration range of 6.5–250 μg/mL. After 48 h, 20 μL of 5 mg/mL MTT was added to the wells of 96-well plate and incubated for 3 h at 37 °C. The medium was removed from the wells and 100 μL of DMSO were added to each well to dissolve formazan crystals. Subsequently, the absorbance was measured at 570 nm with reference at 630 nm using an Infinite NanoQuant M200, Tecan (Switzerland) microplate reader. The following equation was used to determine the percentage of cell viability.
Cell Viability
%
=
A 570
−
A 630
treated cells
A 570
−
A 630
control cell
× 100

Fluorescence microscopy was used to analyze the internalization of CβCD-MSCDs-ETO with FA into HeLa and HepG2 cells. Either HeLa or HepG2 cells were seeded in 12-well plates (density = 5 × 104 cells/well) and incubated at 37 °C under 5% CO2 atmosphere in DMEM (500 μL). Subsequently, the media were substituted with fresh DMEM medium (500 μL) containing 10% FBS. Then, FA-CβCD-MSCDs-ETO (equivalent to 12.5 μg/L of ETO) was added and incubated for 3 h to allow for the cellular uptake to take place. Finally, the media were eliminated, the cells were washed several times with PBS and visualized using inverted Juli Smart fluorescent cell analyzer (South Korea).

Autodock Vina program [42] was used to perform the molecular docking studies by the Lamarckian genetic algorithm [43]. The Top II (PDB ID: 3QX3) crystal structure [44] was taken from the RCSB protein data bank (http://www.pdb.org) with the resolution of 2.16 Å. ETO molecule was first docked to Top II, in which the grid map was set to 22 × 22 × 22 Å3 along the x, y, and z axes with 0.375 Å grid spacing. Subsequently, the lowest binding energy conformation from the first ETO docking was loaded into the Top II. This complex was used for docking of the second ETO molecule. The grid map was set to 22 × 22 × 22 Å3 along the x, y, and z axes with 0.375 Å grid spacing. LIGPLOT+ [45], which is a program for automatically plotting protein-ligand interactions, was used in the analysis of the interactions between Top II and ETO molecules. The UCSF Chimera [46] packages were used to produce the molecular image.

MDSs were performed with the GROMACS 5.4.6 program to study the reliability of the docking results and evaluate the changes in the complex structure over time using the amber 99 force field [47]. Dundee PRODRG2.5 server (beta) was used to generate the topological parameters of two ETO molecules [48, 49]. Next, the Top II with the two ETO molecules were located in a cubic box with dimensions of 13.7 × 13.7 × 13.7 nm3 containing 78,084 water molecules using a simple point charge (SPC216) model [50]. The system was neutralized through substation of 40 solvent molecules with 40 Na+ ions. The steepest descent method of 50,000 steps with a cutoff of 1.0 nm for van der Waals and Coulomb forces was used to carry out the energy minimization steps. An NPT (constant number of molecules, pressure, and temperature) ensemble [51] was used to perform simulations by the Berendsen thermostat [52] with coupled temperature and pressure at 310 K and 1 bar, respectively. The full system was finally exposed to 10 ns MDS at a temperature of 310 K and pressure of 1 bar. The simulation was performed by the periodic boundary condition and the leapfrog algorithm was applied to integrate the motion equations with a time step of 2 fs. The MDSs were performed using an open SUSE 11.3 Linux with an Intel Core 8 Quad Q6600 2.4 GHz and 4 GB of RAM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of FA-CβCD-MSCDs nanoparticles
Drug loading and in vitro release
ce:section.ce:para: The potential application of FA-CβCD-MSCDs as a delivery carrier was evaluated using ETO as a model drug. ETO was encapsulated in nanoformulations with EE and loading content of 72.40% ± 1.03 and 14.49% ± 0.32, respectively. The ETO release experiment was carried out in PBS (pH 7.4) and citrate buffer (pH 5.4) at 37 °C. Only 3.9% of ETO was released from the nanocarrier at pH 7.4 during 72 h (Fig. 4 ), which could be attributed to the encapsulation of ETO inside the silica pores, whereas ETO-loaded MSCDs without CβCD released 8% ETO at pH 7.4 PBS during 12 h (Fig. S7, Supporting information). ETO was released significantly faster at pH 7.4 from ETO-loaded MSCDs compared to FA-CβCD-MSCDs. The results of the release study revealed that the release rate of ETO from the developed formulation is pH-dependent which might enhance the release of drug moiety in tumor tissue and cancer cells with acidic pH compared to blood circulation with physiologic pH (Fig. 4). The aforementioned accelerated release at pH 5.4 could be ascribed to the protonation of amine groups on MSNs which may cause the MSN pores to become hydrophilic thereby leading to the release of ETO as a hydrophobic drug. It should be noted that the behavior of the formulation in vivo might be different due to the presence of hydrolytic enzymes which could further contribute to the faster drug release [28].

Nanosystems should be preprocessed with other entities to assure their lower cell toxicity under the physiological medium for application in drug delivery purposes. Therefore, MTT assay against HeLa and HepG2 cells was used to investigate the cytotoxicity of the prepared nanocarriers (Fig. 5A and B). The cell viability is maintained above 83% after incubation with CDs and FA-CβCD-MSCDs for 48 h at concentrations as high as 250 μg/mL. This demonstrates the low cytotoxicity of the CDs and FA-CβCD-MSCDs. Therefore, the results show that the CDs and FA-CβCD-MSCDs nanocarriers with low cytotoxicity are appropriate for biomedical applications. Furthermore, the cytotoxic effect of the targeted and non-targeted nanoformulations was tested on the HeLa and HepG2 cell lines. The cytotoxicity of free ETO and ETO-loaded CβCD-MSCDs and FA-CβCD-MSCDs are shown in Fig. 5C and D. The sample concentrations were calculated based on the concentration of ETO present in the formulation. The obtained results verified that CβCD-MSCDs-ETO is more cytotoxic than free ETO in HeLa. However, FA-CβCD-MSCDs-ETO is more cytotoxic in the aforementioned cell lines compared to CβCD-MSCDs-ETO. This may be due to the interaction between FA and folate receptors in HeLa cells, which makes the internalization of FA-CβCD-MSCDs-ETO by receptor mediated endocytosis more efficient. However, FA-CβCD-MSCDs-ETO is not significantly more effective against FA-negative HepG2 cells than CβC-MSCDs-ETO. These findings show that FA can specifically target and deliver ETO to FA-positive cells.

Fluorescence microscopy was used to study the cellular uptake of targeted CD-loaded nanosystems in Hela and HepG2 cells. HeLa with overexpressed FRs and HepG2 cells without FRs were chosen as the target and control cells, respectively. HeLa and HepG2 cells were treated with FA-targeted CβCD-MSCDs-ETO (12.5 μg/mL) for 3 h at 37 °C. As observed in Fig. 6 A and B, when the HeLa and HepG2 cells were treated with FA-CβCD-MSCDs-ETO, the fluorescence signal of the HeLa cells was stronger than that of HepG2 cells, showing greater uptake of FA-CβCD-MSCDs-ETO by HeLa cells in comparison with HepG2 cells. Consequently, these cellular imaging results confirm that the pH-responsive FA-CβCD-MSCDs-ETO nanohybrids can enter Hela cells through receptor-mediated endocytosis via folate receptors more easily than the negative HepG2 cells with no FRs.

Molecular docking calculations were applied to investigate the interaction of two ETO molecules with Top II in this work. The docking studies were done in order to define the preferred binding sites of ETO to Top II, find the possible binding conformations of ETO and obtain closer conformations to bioactive ones for MDS studies. Minimized affinity for the first conformer of two ETO molecules with Top II was −13.1 Kcal mol−1. Therefore, the first conformer was selected as the first guess of binding structure for the following MDSs. Fig. 7 B shows the 2D schematic interaction model. There are two hydrogen bonds between the O3 and O4 atoms of ETO I and the amino atom groups of Gln271, DA1353 with 2.68 and 3.22 Å lengths, two hydrogen bonds between the O4, O12 atoms of ETO II and the amino atom groups of DC1373, Arg726 with 3.27, 3.01 Å lengths. As observed in Fig. 7B, there are nine hydrophobic contact points between the ETO I atoms and the Top II and eight hydrophobic contact points between the ETO II atoms and the Top II in the presence of DNA.

MDSs were used for interaction analysis of two ETO molecules with Top II in the presence of DNA. Using the GROMACS 4.5.6, the first complex binding structure guess, which had been obtained by molecular docking simulations, was simulated for 10 ns. The root mean square deviations (RMSD) of the trajectories from their initial structure were used to find out whether or not the complex structure stayed stable under the simulation conditions. The RMSD values of the backbone and two ETO molecules ranged from 0 to 0.51 nm and 0 to 0.096 nm, respectively (Fig. S8). The results indicate that the docked complex structure is stable throughout MDS. The binding interactions and overall view of ETO molecules position on the DNA binding site of Top II are shown in Fig. 7D. The amino acid residues of the Top II and the bases of the DNA lining the binding site of ETO I consist of Gly 478, Leu 502, Arg 503, Gly504, Gln 778 and Met 782, DC 8, DT 9, DG 10, DA 12 and DG 13. The amino acid residues of the Top II and the bases of the DNA lining the binding site of ETO II consist of Glu 1227, Gly 1228, Leu 1252, Arg 1253, Gly 1254, Gln 1471, Ala 1472, Met 1474, Pro1512, DC 8, DT 9, DG 10, DA 12 and DG 13. Furthermore, hydrogen bonds and hydrophobic interaction during the course of trajectories were investigated in detail. Hydrogen bonds and hydrophobic interactions extracted from the trajectory in 1–10 ns of MDS have been summarized in Table 2 (Figs. 7D and S9). The results show that ETO-Top II complex is mainly formed on the basis of hydrophobic interactions, which compensate for the lack of hydrogen bonding interactions in ETO-Top II complex.

The aim of this work was to develop a pH-responsive and label-free fluorescence FA-CβCD-MSCDs for controlled, targeted drug delivery and imaging. CβCD blocks the pores of ETO-encapsulated amine-MSCDs nanohybrids, thereby preventing premature drug leakage and enhancing the cytotoxic activity. FA was successfully attached as a targeting ligand to CβCD-MSCDs to prepare folate decorated nanoparticles based on the interaction between FA and FR. The results of in vitro cell imaging and MTT experiments show that the ETO-loaded FA-CβCD-MSCDs can target tumor cells overexpressing FR and control the drug release by a cleavable pH-responsive CβCD gatekeeper, leading to enhanced cytotoxicity and possibly lower side effects. The present study provides a potentially efficient theranostic platform for the future application of FA-CβCD-MSCDs, which can be used as helpful diagnostic and therapeutic tools. Molecular modeling was then used to reveal the interactions of ETO molecules with the Top II. Two ETO molecules were docked into the candidate pocket of Top II in the first step. In the second step, 10 ns MDSs were carried out to identify the interactions between ETO molecules and Top II. The results show that ETOs-Top II complex forms mainly on the basis of hydrophobic interactions.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to thank Isfahan University of Technology (IUT) Research Council and Center of Excellence in Sensor and Green Chemistry for their support. We gratefully acknowledge the Sheikh Bahaei National High Performance Computing of the Isfahan University of Technology for performing the parallel simulation on RAKHSH cluster.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.msec.2018.07.043.
